content_markdown: Which of the following is **true** when thermal energy is converted into work in a single process and a cyclical process?

diagram_metadata.description: A table comparing the conversion of thermal energy into work for a single process and a cyclical process across four options A, B, C, and D.

options.label: A
options.text: Single process: complete conversion of thermal energy into work can occur; Cyclical process: energy must be transferred from the system

options.label: B
options.text: Single process: complete conversion of thermal energy into work can never occur; Cyclical process: energy must be transferred from the system

options.label: C
options.text: Single process: complete conversion of thermal energy into work can occur; Cyclical process: energy need not be transferred from the system

options.label: D
options.text: Single process: complete conversion of thermal energy into work can never occur; Cyclical process: energy need not be transferred from the system

vibe_explanation: In a single process (like an isothermal expansion), all heat absorbed can be converted into work as long as the internal energy change is zero. However, the Second Law of Thermodynamics (Kelvin-Planck statement) dictates that for a cyclical process, it is impossible to convert all heat from a single reservoir into work; some energy must always be rejected to a cold reservoir (transferred from the system).
